findings.message: RegExp() called with a `extension` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

Detected the use of eval(). eval() can be dangerous if used to evaluate dynamic content. If this content can be input from outside the program, this may be a code injection vulnerability. Ensure evaluated content is not definable by external sources.

Detected string concatenation with a non-literal variable in a util.format / console.log function. If an attacker injects a format specifier in the string, it will forge the log message. Try to use constant values for the format string.

RegExp() called with a `name` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

Detected possible user input going into a `path.join` or `path.resolve` function. This could possibly lead to a path traversal vulnerability,  where the attacker can access arbitrary files stored in the file system. Instead, be sure to sanitize or validate user input first.

Detected string concatenation with a non-literal variable in a util.format / console.log function. If an attacker injects a format specifier in the string, it will forge the log message. Try to use constant values for the format string.

Skill claims to be officially made by, published by, or endorsed by Anthropic or to be an official Claude product. Brand impersonation in skill manifests is a social engineering technique used to gain elevated trust from users and the agent runtime, potentially bypassing skepticism about unusual instructions. Legitimate Anthropic-published skills would not self-identify this way in a third-party skill file. Review the skill's publisher identity and allowed-tools list carefully.

Detected possible user input going into a `path.join` or `path.resolve` function. This could possibly lead to a path traversal vulnerability,  where the attacker can access arbitrary files stored in the file system. Instead, be sure to sanitize or validate user input first.

Skill contains a destructive system command targeting critical filesystem paths or system resources. Commands such as rm -rf on root-level paths, disk wiping (dd, wipefs, shred), unsafe chmod/chown on system directories, PATH hijacking, writes to /etc/shadow or /etc/sudoers, and interactive root shell drops are characteristic of malicious skill payloads. These can cause irreversible data loss, privilege escalation, or full system compromise. Review immediately to confirm this is not a malicious skill.

RegExp() called with a `pattern` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

Legitimate Anthropic-published skills would not self-identify this way in a third-party skill file. Review the skill's publisher identity and allowed-tools list carefully.

Skill contains a destructive system command targeting critical filesystem paths or system resources. Commands such as rm -rf on root-level paths, disk wiping (dd, wipefs, shred), unsafe chmod/chown on system directories, PATH hijacking, writes to /etc/shadow or /etc/sudoers, and interactive root shell drops are characteristic of malicious skill payloads. These can cause irreversible data loss, privilege escalation, or full system compromise. Review immediately to confirm this is not a malicious skill.

RegExp() called with a `pattern` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

RegExp() called with a `patterns` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.
